item.title: How Ethical Sourcing is Changing the Jewellery Game
item.dc:creator: Mr. Mackay
item.content:encoded:
Chain length is another detail worth getting right. A 16-inch chain sits at the collarbone and is ideal for pendants you want to wear close to the neckline. An 18-inch chain falls just below the collarbone — the most universally flattering length for everyday wear. A 20-inch chain rests at the top of the chest and works well for larger, bolder pendants. Tennis necklaces are most commonly worn at 16 or 17 inches for a snug, classic fit. If you are unsure, 18 inches is almost always the right choice.

White Irises

Marty Randell

Cherry Blossom

Marty Randell

Diamonds have been treasured for thousands of years, but the way we understand and evaluate them today is the product of a remarkably precise science. Since the mid-twentieth century, gemologists have used a standardised framework — universally known as the Four Cs — to describe the quality of any diamond. Understanding these four properties will not turn you into an expert overnight, but it will give you the vocabulary and confidence to ask the right questions, compare stones fairly, and ultimately walk away with a piece you are proud to own.

Understanding the Four Cs

The Four Cs are Cut, Colour, Clarity, and Carat weight. Each plays a distinct role in determining how a diamond looks and how much it costs.

  • Often considered the most important of the four, cut refers not to the shape of the diamond but to how well its facets interact with light. A well-cut stone will appear brilliantly alive; a poorly cut one will look dull regardless of its other qualities. Cut grades typically run from Excellent down to Poor.
  • Diamond colour is graded on a scale from D (completely colourless) to Z (noticeably yellow or brown). For most necklace settings in white gold or platinum, diamonds in the D–G range appear beautifully white to the naked eye. In yellow gold settings, slightly warmer stones in the H–J range can look equally stunning at a more accessible price point.
  • Clarity measures the presence of internal inclusions or external blemishes. The scale runs from Flawless (FL) to Included (I3). For a pendant worn at the chest, a stone graded VS2 or SI1 will almost certainly appear eye-clean — meaning no imperfections are visible without magnification — making it an excellent value choice.
  • Carat is simply a measure of weight (one carat equals 0.2 grams). Larger stones are rarer and therefore more expensive per carat, but carat weight alone does not determine beauty. A smaller, well-cut diamond will consistently outshine a larger, poorly cut one.

Once you have a handle on the Four Cs, the next question is style. Diamond necklaces span an enormous range of designs, and understanding the most popular silhouettes will help you match the piece to its intended purpose — and to the person who will wear it.

A solitaire pendant is the most enduringly popular choice. A single diamond — round brilliant, oval, or pear-shaped — suspended from a fine chain is effortlessly versatile. It works with a casual weekend outfit and a black-tie gown in equal measure, which makes it an ideal everyday piece and a safe choice when you are buying for someone else. A halo pendant adds a ring of smaller diamonds around a central stone, dramatically amplifying the perceived size and sparkle for its price. For those who want something truly lavish, a tennis necklace — a continuous line of identically matched diamonds encircling the neck — is the pinnacle of diamond jewellery. It is a piece that genuinely needs no other jewellery to complete a look.

Thank you for taking the time to read this post. Stay tuned for more updates!

item.title: About us
item.dc:creator: Mr. Mackay
item.content:encoded:
HomePage

Welcome to WoodMart Jewelry Store

We are passionate about crafting timeless and elegant jewelry that celebrates life’s special moments. Our collections are designed with meticulous attention to detail and a commitment to quality, ensuring each piece is a work of art. Whether you’re looking for a statement piece or a subtle touch of sophistication, our jewelry is crafted to make every occasion memorable. Discover our range and experience the beauty of exquisite craftsmanship and thoughtful design.

Thank you for choosing us to be a part of your journey.

Necklaces / Bracelets / Accessories / Rings / Sets / Earrings / Necklaces / Bracelets / Accessories / Rings / Sets / Earrings / Necklaces / Bracelets / Accessories / Rings / Sets / Earrings / Necklaces / Bracelets / Accessories / Rings / Sets / Earrings /

“WoodMart Jewelry combines elegance and craftsmanship, offering timeless pieces that belong in every jewelry collection.”

#WDJEWELLERY

5549 795 7228 549 1572 43 2576 274 9959 977 Show it to the world on Instagram #WDJEWELLERY @WOODMARTJEWELLERY

item.title: Contact us
item.dc:creator: Mr. Mackay
item.content:encoded:
HomePage

Contact us

If you have any questions or need general information, please fill out this form to request the details you’re looking for. We’d be honored to assist you.

item.title: Meet the Artisans Who Bring Our Jewellery to Life
item.dc:creator: Mr. Mackay
item.content:encoded:
Chain length is another detail worth getting right. A 16-inch chain sits at the collarbone and is ideal for pendants you want to wear close to the neckline. An 18-inch chain falls just below the collarbone — the most universally flattering length for everyday wear. A 20-inch chain rests at the top of the chest and works well for larger, bolder pendants. Tennis necklaces are most commonly worn at 16 or 17 inches for a snug, classic fit. If you are unsure, 18 inches is almost always the right choice.

White Irises

Marty Randell

Cherry Blossom

Marty Randell

Diamonds have been treasured for thousands of years, but the way we understand and evaluate them today is the product of a remarkably precise science. Since the mid-twentieth century, gemologists have used a standardised framework — universally known as the Four Cs — to describe the quality of any diamond. Understanding these four properties will not turn you into an expert overnight, but it will give you the vocabulary and confidence to ask the right questions, compare stones fairly, and ultimately walk away with a piece you are proud to own.

Understanding the Four Cs

The Four Cs are Cut, Colour, Clarity, and Carat weight. Each plays a distinct role in determining how a diamond looks and how much it costs.

  • Often considered the most important of the four, cut refers not to the shape of the diamond but to how well its facets interact with light. A well-cut stone will appear brilliantly alive; a poorly cut one will look dull regardless of its other qualities. Cut grades typically run from Excellent down to Poor.
  • Diamond colour is graded on a scale from D (completely colourless) to Z (noticeably yellow or brown). For most necklace settings in white gold or platinum, diamonds in the D–G range appear beautifully white to the naked eye. In yellow gold settings, slightly warmer stones in the H–J range can look equally stunning at a more accessible price point.
  • Clarity measures the presence of internal inclusions or external blemishes. The scale runs from Flawless (FL) to Included (I3). For a pendant worn at the chest, a stone graded VS2 or SI1 will almost certainly appear eye-clean — meaning no imperfections are visible without magnification — making it an excellent value choice.
  • Carat is simply a measure of weight (one carat equals 0.2 grams). Larger stones are rarer and therefore more expensive per carat, but carat weight alone does not determine beauty. A smaller, well-cut diamond will consistently outshine a larger, poorly cut one.

Once you have a handle on the Four Cs, the next question is style. Diamond necklaces span an enormous range of designs, and understanding the most popular silhouettes will help you match the piece to its intended purpose — and to the person who will wear it.

A solitaire pendant is the most enduringly popular choice. A single diamond — round brilliant, oval, or pear-shaped — suspended from a fine chain is effortlessly versatile. It works with a casual weekend outfit and a black-tie gown in equal measure, which makes it an ideal everyday piece and a safe choice when you are buying for someone else. A halo pendant adds a ring of smaller diamonds around a central stone, dramatically amplifying the perceived size and sparkle for its price. For those who want something truly lavish, a tennis necklace — a continuous line of identically matched diamonds encircling the neck — is the pinnacle of diamond jewellery. It is a piece that genuinely needs no other jewellery to complete a look.

Thank you for taking the time to read this post. Stay tuned for more updates!

Marty Randell

Cherry Blossom

Marty Randell

A well-cut stone will appear brilliantly alive; a poorly cut one will look dull regardless of its other qualities. Cut grades typically run from Excellent down to Poor.

For a pendant worn at the chest, a stone graded VS2 or SI1 will almost certainly appear eye-clean — meaning no imperfections are visible without magnification — making it an excellent value choice.

item.title: Finding the Perfect Piece for Every Occasion
item.dc:creator: Mr. Mackay
item.content:encoded:
Chain length is another detail worth getting right. A 16-inch chain sits at the collarbone and is ideal for pendants you want to wear close to the neckline. An 18-inch chain falls just below the collarbone — the most universally flattering length for everyday wear. A 20-inch chain rests at the top of the chest and works well for larger, bolder pendants. Tennis necklaces are most commonly worn at 16 or 17 inches for a snug, classic fit. If you are unsure, 18 inches is almost always the right choice.

White Irises

Marty Randell

Cherry Blossom

Marty Randell

Diamonds have been treasured for thousands of years, but the way we understand and evaluate them today is the product of a remarkably precise science. Since the mid-twentieth century, gemologists have used a standardised framework — universally known as the Four Cs — to describe the quality of any diamond. Understanding these four properties will not turn you into an expert overnight, but it will give you the vocabulary and confidence to ask the right questions, compare stones fairly, and ultimately walk away with a piece you are proud to own.

Understanding the Four Cs

The Four Cs are Cut, Colour, Clarity, and Carat weight. Each plays a distinct role in determining how a diamond looks and how much it costs.

  • Often considered the most important of the four, cut refers not to the shape of the diamond but to how well its facets interact with light. A well-cut stone will appear brilliantly alive; a poorly cut one will look dull regardless of its other qualities. Cut grades typically run from Excellent down to Poor.
  • Diamond colour is graded on a scale from D (completely colourless) to Z (noticeably yellow or brown). For most necklace settings in white gold or platinum, diamonds in the D–G range appear beautifully white to the naked eye. In yellow gold settings, slightly warmer stones in the H–J range can look equally stunning at a more accessible price point.
  • Clarity measures the presence of internal inclusions or external blemishes. The scale runs from Flawless (FL) to Included (I3). For a pendant worn at the chest, a stone graded VS2 or SI1 will almost certainly appear eye-clean — meaning no imperfections are visible without magnification — making it an excellent value choice.
  • Carat is simply a measure of weight (one carat equals 0.2 grams). Larger stones are rarer and therefore more expensive per carat, but carat weight alone does not determine beauty. A smaller, well-cut diamond will consistently outshine a larger, poorly cut one.

Once you have a handle on the Four Cs, the next question is style. Diamond necklaces span an enormous range of designs, and understanding the most popular silhouettes will help you match the piece to its intended purpose — and to the person who will wear it.

A solitaire pendant is the most enduringly popular choice. A single diamond — round brilliant, oval, or pear-shaped — suspended from a fine chain is effortlessly versatile. It works with a casual weekend outfit and a black-tie gown in equal measure, which makes it an ideal everyday piece and a safe choice when you are buying for someone else. A halo pendant adds a ring of smaller diamonds around a central stone, dramatically amplifying the perceived size and sparkle for its price. For those who want something truly lavish, a tennis necklace — a continuous line of identically matched diamonds encircling the neck — is the pinnacle of diamond jewellery. It is a piece that genuinely needs no other jewellery to complete a look.

Thank you for taking the time to read this post. Stay tuned for more updates!

item.content:encoded:

An 18-inch chain falls just below the collarbone — the most universally flattering length for everyday wear. A 20-inch chain rests at the top of the chest and works well for larger, bolder pendants. Tennis necklaces are most commonly worn at 16 or 17 inches for a snug, classic fit. If you are unsure, 18 inches is almost always the right choice.

Marty Randell

Cherry Blossom

Marty Randell

Diamonds have been treasured for thousands of years, but the way we understand and evaluate them today is the product of a remarkably precise science. Since the mid-twentieth century, gemologists have used a standardised framework — universally known as the Four Cs — to describe the quality of any diamond. Understanding these four properties will not turn you into an expert overnight, but it will give you the vocabulary and confidence to ask the right questions, compare stones fairly, and ultimately walk away with a piece you are proud to own.

A well-cut stone will appear brilliantly alive; a poorly cut one will look dull regardless of its other qualities. Cut grades typically run from Excellent down to Poor.
  • Diamond colour is graded on a scale from D (completely colourless) to Z (noticeably yellow or brown). For most necklace settings in white gold or platinum, diamonds in the D–G range appear beautifully white to the naked eye. In yellow gold settings, slightly warmer stones in the H–J range can look equally stunning at a more accessible price point.
  • Clarity measures the presence of internal inclusions or external blemishes. The scale runs from Flawless (FL) to Included (I3). For a pendant worn at the chest, a stone graded VS2 or SI1 will almost certainly appear eye-clean — meaning no imperfections are visible without magnification — making it an excellent value choice.
  • Carat is simply a measure of weight (one carat equals 0.2 grams). Larger stones are rarer and therefore more expensive per carat, but carat weight alone does not determine beauty. A smaller, well-cut diamond will consistently outshine a larger, poorly cut one.

Once you have a handle on the Four Cs, the next question is style. Diamond necklaces span an enormous range of designs, and understanding the most popular silhouettes will help you match the piece to its intended purpose — and to the person who will wear it.

item.title: The Ultimate Guide to Choosing Your Perfect Diamond Necklace
item.dc:creator: Mr. Mackay
item.content:encoded:
Chain length is another detail worth getting right. A 16-inch chain sits at the collarbone and is ideal for pendants you want to wear close to the neckline. An 18-inch chain falls just below the collarbone — the most universally flattering length for everyday wear. A 20-inch chain rests at the top of the chest and works well for larger, bolder pendants. Tennis necklaces are most commonly worn at 16 or 17 inches for a snug, classic fit. If you are unsure, 18 inches is almost always the right choice.

White Irises

Marty Randell

Cherry Blossom

Marty Randell

Diamonds have been treasured for thousands of years, but the way we understand and evaluate them today is the product of a remarkably precise science. Since the mid-twentieth century, gemologists have used a standardised framework — universally known as the Four Cs — to describe the quality of any diamond. Understanding these four properties will not turn you into an expert overnight, but it will give you the vocabulary and confidence to ask the right questions, compare stones fairly, and ultimately walk away with a piece you are proud to own.

Understanding the Four Cs

The Four Cs are Cut, Colour, Clarity, and Carat weight. Each plays a distinct role in determining how a diamond looks and how much it costs.

  • Often considered the most important of the four, cut refers not to the shape of the diamond but to how well its facets interact with light. A well-cut stone will appear brilliantly alive; a poorly cut one will look dull regardless of its other qualities. Cut grades typically run from Excellent down to Poor.
  • Diamond colour is graded on a scale from D (completely colourless) to Z (noticeably yellow or brown). For most necklace settings in white gold or platinum, diamonds in the D–G range appear beautifully white to the naked eye. In yellow gold settings, slightly warmer stones in the H–J range can look equally stunning at a more accessible price point.
  • Clarity measures the presence of internal inclusions or external blemishes. The scale runs from Flawless (FL) to Included (I3). For a pendant worn at the chest, a stone graded VS2 or SI1 will almost certainly appear eye-clean — meaning no imperfections are visible without magnification — making it an excellent value choice.
  • Carat is simply a measure of weight (one carat equals 0.2 grams). Larger stones are rarer and therefore more expensive per carat, but carat weight alone does not determine beauty. A smaller, well-cut diamond will consistently outshine a larger, poorly cut one.

Once you have a handle on the Four Cs, the next question is style. Diamond necklaces span an enormous range of designs, and understanding the most popular silhouettes will help you match the piece to its intended purpose — and to the person who will wear it.

A solitaire pendant is the most enduringly popular choice. A single diamond — round brilliant, oval, or pear-shaped — suspended from a fine chain is effortlessly versatile. It works with a casual weekend outfit and a black-tie gown in equal measure, which makes it an ideal everyday piece and a safe choice when you are buying for someone else. A halo pendant adds a ring of smaller diamonds around a central stone, dramatically amplifying the perceived size and sparkle for its price. For those who want something truly lavish, a tennis necklace — a continuous line of identically matched diamonds encircling the neck — is the pinnacle of diamond jewellery. It is a piece that genuinely needs no other jewellery to complete a look.

Thank you for taking the time to read this post. Stay tuned for more updates!

item.content:encoded:
Size guide

Size UK US EU Japan
XS 6 - 8 4 34 7
S 8 -10 6 36 9
M 10 - 12 8 38 11
L 12 - 14 10 40 13
XL 14 - 16 12 42 15

item.title: Gift packaging available
item.dc:creator: Mr. Mackay
item.content:encoded:
All Items in One Box

This is the default shipping option. Without gift packaging, multiple items will be sustainably packaged in one box without gift bag or notecard.

Items in individual boxes

  • Far far away, behind the word mountains, far las.
  • An individual jewelry box
  • A gift notecard with personalized message

Your gifts will be individually placed in our signature Pandora box with a gift bag and notecard with an optional personalized gift message, all for $1.00 each gift.

item.title: Shipping & Delivery
item.dc:creator: Mr. Mackay
item.content:encoded:
Free shipping on qualifying orders

Your order will arrive in 2-4 business days. Please allow one additional business day for processing of Engraving orders.

Free standard shipping on select Lab-grown Diamonds. Express orders arrive in 1-2 business days.

What are your shipping options and delivery times?

Orders ship Monday to Friday excluding US Federal holidays.

How do I track my order?

Once your order has shipped from our warehouse, you will receive an email notification with a tracking information. Please also check your junk email. This email will have all your shipping information and ways to track your order.

What if I received an incorrect item or an item is missing from my order?

We apologize for any inconvenience if you are missing items from your order or received the incorrect item. If this should happen please contact our Customer Care team immediately so we can resolve the issue.

Will ship products to locations outside of the U.S.?

US Online ships within the United States (including Alaska, Hawaii, and Puerto Rico). Unfortunately, we do not deliver to American Samoa, U.S. Virgin Islands, Guam, Micronesia, Marshall Islands, Northern Mariana Islands, or Palau.
